query: can fine-needle aspiration cytology distinguish between nonproliferative and proliferative breast disease without atypia

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Clinical Significance -- Disadvantages
retrieved_docs.content: Lower diagnostic yield than core needle biopsy Four percent to 13% of samples found to be inadequate or nondiagnostic More difficult to distinguish invasive versus in-situ carcinoma May require more experienced cytopathologist for diagnosis Non-diagnostic results necessitate repeat biopsy (typically CNB or vacuum-assisted)

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Clinical Significance -- Accuracy
retrieved_docs.content: Certain studies have cited the sensitivity of aspiration cytology for malignancy at 64% for one aspiration sample and 91% in patients with three samples. Specificity was 56% (inadequate or unsatisfactory cytological preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Clinical Significance -- Advantages of FNA over other Modalities
retrieved_docs.content: Cost-effective (declines with inconclusive results/need for repeat biopsies) Less invasive/less painful secondary to smaller needle size Can be performed in the clinic or office Appropriate for patients who are being anticoagulated with low risk of hematoma/bleeding Can be used to sample axillary tissue

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Indications -- Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: FNA is indicated for patients with a mammographic abnormality or palpable breast lesions. FNA remains controversial for the diagnosis of suspected malignant lesions due to the relatively small amount of breast tissue that is sampled and the high rate of non-diagnostic or inadequate samples. In this case, the “triple test” is performed using a combination of the physical exam, imaging studies and cytology to make this diagnosis to increase diagnostic accuracy. Though the Z0011 study is changing the treatment of axillary disease, axillary ultrasound with FNA sampling of suspicious appearing nodes is advisable. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Lobular Breast Carcinoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The challenge with invasive lobular carcinomas is that they can be difficult to detect clinically and radiographically, and consequently tend to be larger at the time of diagnosis. They are often poorly circumscribed and can be missed on fine-needle aspiration and needle core biopsies due to low cellularity and the bland nature of the tumor cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Clinical Significance -- Follow-up
retrieved_docs.content: If the FNA reveals a non-bloody aspirate and the cyst resolves, no more follow up is required. Some physicians do follow up with an ultrasound to ensure that the cyst has completely resolved. Re-evaluation is usually done 4 to 6 weeks later. If the cyst persists after aspiration or contains bloody fluid, the patient should be referred to a surgeon for a formal biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Ductal Hyperplasia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: It is also crucial that surgeons know the appropriate surgical management of this finding. ADH on core needle biopsy will require needle or seed localized excisional biopsy to rule out nearby breast carcinoma. However, if ADH is found on excisional biopsy to be ADH only - even if margins are positive - the patient is surgically complete. [Level 2]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Breast Hyperplasia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 10% of female breast biopsies will contain an atypical proliferative lesion, with most of these lesions found in women in their 40s.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Breast Hyperplasia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Important differential diagnostic entities to consider when viewing possible ADH or ALH include usual ductal hyperplasia, ductal carcinoma in situ, lobular carcinoma in situ, and flat epithelial atypia, with the most important distinguisher being whether a lesion is ADH or low-grade DCIS. These distinctions can be made on the histologic features as outlined above, noting that there are also difficult cases where particularly problematic borderline lesions may be diagnosed as “ADH bordering on DCIS” or “ADH suspicious for DCIS.” [11] [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Ductal Hyperplasia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: If a core biopsy identifies atypical ductal hyperplasia, it is important to take a larger excisional biopsy to ensure a large enough specimen to rule out carcinoma of the breast. It is also important to note that on biopsy, low-grade DCIS and ADH share many morphologic similarities. Therefore the pathology specimen at hand should be carefully examined to rule out DCIS. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Ductal Hyperplasia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Large nuclei with conspicuous nucleoli and frequent mitosis, such as those seen in intermediate or high-grade DCIS, are never present in ADH. If high-grade nuclei are identified, regardless of size, a diagnosis of high-grade DCIS is rendered.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Ductal Hyperplasia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical ductal hyperplasia is a pathology finding, usually found incidentally on biopsy of the breast. The diagnosis by itself is not a precancerous or cancerous lesion. It is, however, a high-risk lesion, indicating the presence of ADH on pathology flags the patient as one who is fivefold more likely to develop breast carcinoma - in any area of the breasts - in the future. For this reason, the nuance of the cancer risk of the finding of ADH on pathology is more challenging to communicate with the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Ductal Hyperplasia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathology findings typical of atypical ductal hyperplasia. Explain the impact of the diagnosis on breast cancer risk. Outline the surgical risk modification recommendations based on the diagnosis. Outline the medical risk modification recommendations based on the diagnosis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Breast Hyperplasia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: ADH is characterized by an atypical monomorphic epithelial population of luminal origin within the breast that is between normal/usual hyperplasia and low-grade ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) on the spectrum of intraductal proliferative lesions. ADH will share some of the features of DCIS without meeting the full criteria for low-grade DCIS. The architectural features of low-grade DCIS include cribriform spaces with polarized lumens composed of highly monotonous cells with fine chromatin in a solid, cribriform, or micropapillary pattern.

retrieved_docs.title: Fine Needle Aspiration of Breast Masses -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle aspiration of breast masses is a type of biopsy performed using a small needle to obtain samples of tissue and fluid from solid or cystic breast lesions. It is one of the many different modalities for diagnosing breast masses. This activity reviews the indications, contraindications, and technique involved in performing fine needle aspiration of breast masses and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the care of patients undergoing this procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Breast Hyperplasia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical hyperplasia of the breast is defined as abnormal epithelial proliferative breast lesions that are not qualitatively or quantitatively abnormal enough to be classified as carcinoma in situ. Atypical hyperplasia is a premalignant condition and may occur in either ductal or lobular epithelium. This activity describes the evaluation, surveillance, and management of atypical breast hyperplasia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the care of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1976
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed Biopsy of lesion of areola is classified as a خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy) and is performed directly on the areola, which serves as the direct procedural site of the biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 896
retrieved_docs.content: The observation that a biopsy of the breast shows a malignant neoplasm is a specific instance of سرطان الثدي (breast cancer). This relationship highlights that histopathologic confirmation of malignant cells in breast tissue directly defines the presence of breast cancer, which is critical for accurate diagnosis and subsequent treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2207
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola is a specific form of خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy). The broader category described as breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on the breast includes خزعة الثدي as one of its constituent procedures. Consequently, خزعة الثدي represents a diagnostic intervention within the spectrum of breast procedures, and the areolar lesion biopsy is a particular application of this technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1533
retrieved_docs.content: The areola is identified as the direct site where a biopsy of a lesion is performed, and it is also recognized as a location where breast cancer may be found, highlighting its importance in diagnostic evaluation of potential breast malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1947
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola and biopsy of lesion of nipple are both classified as خزعة الثدي, indicating that these procedures are specific forms of breast biopsy that target lesions of the areola and nipple respectively. Recognizing these as subtypes of خزعة الثدي helps clinicians select appropriate diagnostic approaches for lesions located in these distinct anatomical regions of the breast.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 941
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola is classified as a type of خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy). The procedure described as biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm is also a form of خزعة الثدي, and when this biopsy reveals a malignant neoplasm it corresponds to سرطان الثدي (breast cancer). Additionally, the broader category “breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast” encompasses خزعة الثدي as a specific subset. Together, these relationships illustrate that specific breast biopsy techniques—including biopsies of areolar lesions and biopsies that detect malignant neoplasms—are instances of خزعة الثدي, that malignant findings on such biopsies indicate سرطان الثدي, and that خزعة الثدي itself is part of the larger group of breast diagnostic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is a specific outcome of a breast biopsy (خزعة الثدي) and serves as a diagnostic indicator of breast cancer (سرطان الثدي). In other words, when a breast biopsy reveals a malignant neoplasm, it confirms the presence of breast cancer. Moreover, “breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast” (خزعة الثدي) represents the broader category of breast tissue sampling and related diagnostic techniques, of which the malignant‑neoplasm finding is one particular result. This relationship highlights how breast biopsy procedures are used to detect malignant disease and how the detection of a malignant neoplasm directly links the procedure to a diagnosis of breast cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of areola is a type of خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy) that is performed directly on the areola, making the areola the direct procedure site for this specific lesion biopsy. Similarly, the statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” also falls under the category of خزعة الثدي, indicating that a breast biopsy can reveal malignant pathology. Together, these concepts illustrate that breast biopsies (خزعة الثدي) may be targeted to distinct anatomical regions such as the areola and can provide critical diagnostic information when a malignant neoplasm is identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: The statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is identified as a خزعة الثدي (breast biopsy). This indicates that a tissue sample taken from the breast has revealed a malignant tumor, confirming the presence of breast cancer. Recognizing this finding as a type of breast biopsy is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of an appropriate treatment plan.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2388
retrieved_docs.content: Biopsy of lesion of nipple is classified as a خزعة الثدي, meaning it is a type of breast biopsy performed to evaluate suspicious nipple lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2495
retrieved_docs.content: "Biopsy of lesion of areola" is classified as a type of "خزعة الثدي" (breast biopsy). Likewise, "biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm" is also a form of "خزعة الثدي". When a breast biopsy reveals a malignant neoplasm, it indicates "سرطان الثدي" (breast cancer). Thus, breast biopsies—whether of an areolar lesion or of breast tissue—serve as diagnostic procedures that can detect malignant neoplasms, which are synonymous with breast cancer, underscoring the essential role of "خزعة الثدي" in confirming the presence of "سرطان الثدي".

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2560
retrieved_docs.content: A biopsy of a lesion of the areola is classified as a breast biopsy (خزعة الثدي), indicating that tissue sampling from the areolar region is encompassed within the broader category of breast tissue biopsy procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1044
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is classified as a خزعة الثدي, indicating that this specific finding represents an instance of a breast biopsy. Conversely, the broader category “breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast” includes خزعة الثدي, showing that the Arabic term for breast biopsy falls within the larger set of breast‑focused diagnostic interventions. This hierarchical relationship clarifies that a malignant breast biopsy is a particular type of breast biopsy, and that خزعة الثدي denotes the procedure within the overall spectrum of breast diagnostic procedures, aiding accurate documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: The procedures described as “Biopsy of lesion of areola” and “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” are both classified as breast biopsies (خزعة الثدي). These statements indicate that a biopsy targeting an areolar lesion and a biopsy that reveals a malignant breast neoplasm are each specific instances of the broader diagnostic technique of خزعة الثدي, which is used to obtain tissue samples from the breast for pathological evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1059
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” denotes a pathological result obtained from a breast biopsy (خزعة الثدي) and serves as a definitive indicator of breast cancer (سرطان الثدي). This relationship highlights that when a breast tissue sample reveals a malignant neoplasm, it confirms the presence of breast cancer, linking the diagnostic procedure directly to the disease entity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure termed “Biopsy of lesion of areola” is performed directly on the areola, indicating that the areola is the specific anatomic site of this intervention. This biopsy is classified as a type of “خزعة الثدي,” the Arabic term for breast biopsy, placing it within the spectrum of breast tissue sampling techniques. Moreover, “خزعة الثدي” itself is encompassed by the broader category of “breast biopsy and other diagnostic procedures on breast,” which includes all diagnostic interventions performed on breast tissue. Together, these relationships clarify that a biopsy of an areolar lesion is a specialized breast biopsy directed at the areola and that breast biopsies, including this specific procedure, belong to the larger group of breast diagnostic procedures.
